channel.title: NYT > Movies
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Casting, Which Will Be Celebrated at This Year’s Oscars, Has Changed Drastically
item.description: What once involved an actor and a casting director in a room is now a technologically advanced exercise with pros and cons for performers.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman

item.title: ‘The Bride!’ Review: Jessie Buckley and Christian Bale Are Outlaws in Love
item.description: Maggie Gyllenhaal’s time-shifting, genre-hopping riff on Mary Shelley’s creation stars Jessie Buckley and Christian Bale as outlaws in love.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: ‘Hoppers’ Review: Animal Magnetism
item.description: The latest Pixar movie centers on pudgy beavers and a spunky teenager determined to save their habitat.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘Fukushima: A Nuclear Nightmare’ Review: An Emotional Choice
item.description: This documentary offers a rundown of the 2011 nuclear meltdown with special attention to the shift workers who risked their lives while trying to stabilize the plant.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: Timothée Chalamet Has a Point About Ballet
item.description: This art form is alive, and Chalamet, who comes from a dance family, knows it. But what value does ballet have for the world at large?
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas
item.media:description: Timothée Chalamet at a Knicks game in 2019. He said this week, “I don’t want to be working in ballet or opera, or, you know, things where it’s like, ‘Hey, keep this thing alive.’”

item.title: A Film That Makes a Strong Argument for the Value of Debate
item.description: “Immutable” is about young debaters in a league in Washington, D.C., as well as about the skill itself in a world where yelling can seem the norm.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Three 6 Mafia on Their Oscars Win, 20 Years Later
item.description: Twenty years ago, Three 6 Mafia’s Oscars triumph for “It’s Hard Out Here for a Pimp” helped an uptight awards show loosen up.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:description: The members of Three 6 Mafia celebrating their win onstage at the Oscars in 2006.

item.title: Did You Know These Oscar-Nominated Actresses Started in Reality TV?
item.description: Hollywood careers don’t always begin where you expect them to, as Jessie Buckley, Teyana Taylor and Emma Stone could tell you.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan

item.title: Bill Kurtis to Leave NPR’s ‘Wait Wait … Don’t Tell Me!’
item.description: As the irreverent quiz show’s judge and scorekeeper, Kurtis, a former news anchor known for his rich voice, has helped find comedy in current events since 2014.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: Bill Kurtis’s introductions on “Wait Wait … Don’t Tell Me!” have included “I’m the voice so creamy, you better take some Lactaid.”

item.title: Michelle Pfeiffer Makes It Look Easy. It Isn’t.
item.description: At 67, the actress is still reinventing herself, with two new TV shows, “The Madison” and “Margo’s Got Money Troubles,” and an evolving attitude about her work.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Amy Harrity
item.media:credit: Amy Harrity for The New York Times

item.title: How the Oscars Best Picture Winner Is Chosen
item.description: Best picture is the only Oscar category decided by preferential ballot. Our reporter Marc Tracy explains how that works.
item.dc:creator: Marc Tracy, Léo Hamelin and Laura Salaberry

item.title: ‘Testament of Ann Lee’ and the Entwining of Dance and God
item.description: “The Testament of Ann Lee” and Shaker traditions represent how movement has been a constantly evolving conduit to heaven.
item.dc:creator: Margaret Fuhrer

item.title: Bobby Cannavale Loves ‘Heated Rivalry’ and His Bearded Dragon
item.description: “I spend an hour a day quietly with this guy, whether it’s feeding him, cleaning out the tank, having him chill with me,” the actor said.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Alan Trustman, Lawyer Who Wrote ‘Thomas Crown Affair,’ Dies at 95
item.description: In a wide-ranging career, he was a member of a Boston white-shoe firm, a Swiss currency trader and a Hollywood screenwriter (“Bullitt” was another of his scripts).
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: Jeremy Larner, 88, Dies; Wrote ‘The Candidate,’ a Political Film Classic
item.description: His Oscar-winning 1972 screenplay starred Robert Redford as an idealistic public interest lawyer making a run for the Senate.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: Tracking Down a Vase From ‘Bonjour Tristesse’
item.description: We help a reader find a prop seen in Durga Chew-Bose’s debut film “Bonjour Tristesse.”
item.dc:creator: Tom Delavan

item.title: From 1962: Brilliant Stardom and Personal Tragedy Punctuated the Life of Marilyn Monroe
item.description: One of the most famous stars in Hollywood, she suffered severe setbacks in the last years of her life.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: From 1981: Death Ends a 39-Year Career, Starting With Child Roles at 4
item.description: Natalie Wood evolved from a child star into a teenage ingénue and then a mature actress, until her trajectory was tragically cut short.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden

item.title: From 1992: Marlene Dietrich, 90, Symbol of Glamour, Dies
item.description: “Dietrich is something that never existed before and may never exist again,” the actor Maurice Chevalier said of her. “That’s a woman.”
item.dc:creator: Peter B. Flint
item.media:credit: Eugene Robert Richee/John Kobal Foundation, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Marlene Dietrich in 1935. She was an international symbol of glamour and sex for more than half a century.

item.title: From 1969: Judy Garland, 47, Found Dead
item.description: She dazzled audiences in “The Wizard of Oz” and “A Star Is Born,” but her successes were later overshadowed by addiction and other struggles.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times

item.title: From 2019: Agnès Varda, Influential French New Wave Filmmaker, Dies at 90
item.description: She was closely associated with the film movement known as the New Wave, although her reimagining of cinematic conventions predated it.
item.dc:creator: John Anderson

item.title: From 2000: Hedy Lamarr, Sultry Star Who Reigned in Hollywood
item.description: A temptress on the silver screen in the 1930s and ’40s, she later became an inventor.
item.dc:creator: Richard Severo
item.media:credit: Clarence Sinclair Bull/Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Hedy Lamarr posing for a promotional portrait for the 1944 film “The Heavenly Body.”

item.title: From 1975: Josephine Baker Is Dead in Paris at 68
item.description: She performed with a string of bananas tied around her waist, an electrifying act that led her to become first a local sensation in Paris, and then an international star.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times

item.title: From 1961: Anna May Wong Is Dead at 54; Actress Won Movie Fame in ’24
item.description: With a film career spanning three decades, Miss Wong, who rose to stardom with “The Thief of Baghdad,” was acclaimed as a versatile and talented performer.
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press
item.media:credit: Daily Herald Archive/National Science & Media Museum/SSPL, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Miss Wong in 1934. She made her first trip to China two years later.

item.title: From 2003: Leni Riefenstahl, Filmmaker and Nazi Propagandist, Dies at 101
item.description: Her documentaries earned her acclaim as a cinematic genius, as well as criticism for putting her talent at the service of Hitler.
item.dc:creator: Alan Riding
item.media:credit: DB Walter Frentz Archiv/picture-alliance/dpa, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Leni Riefenstahl in 1936, the year that the German Olympic Committee commissioned her to record the Berlin Olympics.

item.title: Five International Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include a Mexican drama, a Japanese crime thriller, an absurdist French comedy and more.
item.dc:creator: Devika Girish
item.media:description: Luisa Huertas in “We Shall Not Be Moved.”

item.title: 8 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: ‘War Machine’ Review: On the Fritz
item.description: The “Reacher” star Alan Ritchson can’t troubleshoot this alien invasion sci-fi flick.
item.dc:creator: Robert Daniels

item.title: Wallace Shawn’s ‘What We Did Before Our Moth Days’ Is Purgatory Done Right
item.description: The playwright and his collaborator André Gregory are together again, delivering a sumptuous set of interlinked monologues about life, death and betrayal.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: What We Did Before Our Moth Days (Play)
item.media:description: From left, Hope Davis, John Early, Josh Hamilton and Maria Dizzia in “What We Did Before Our Moth Days” at the Greenwich House Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: ‘Pompei: Below the Clouds’ Review: Trembling Beauties, Old and New
item.description: The past and the present converge in this ravishingly beautiful Italian documentary set in the shadow of Mount Vesuvius.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: ‘Protector’ Review: She Knows How to Give Life, and How to Take It
item.description: Milla Jovovich plays a mother whose wrath is unleashed when her daughter is kidnapped in this brooding revenge thriller.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: Druski Talks About His Influences, Hollywood Ambitions and Timothée Chalamet
item.description: The comedian is building a galaxy of collaborators — Timothée Chalamet, Kai Cenat and Justin Bieber — and telling us how they fit into his future.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica and Joe Coscarelli
item.media:credit: Jason Nocito for The New York Times

item.title: Chadwick Boseman’s Hip-Hop Play, in Shakespeare’s House
item.description: The actor Chadwick Boseman was a playwright, too. At Shakespeare’s Globe in London, his “Deep Azure” is drawing attention to a lost talent inspired by the Bard.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Magdalena Wosinska for The New York Times
item.media:description: Boseman studied theater at Howard University before becoming an actor.

item.title: ‘Youngblood’ Review: He’s Got a Reason to Be Chippy
item.description: A remake of the 1986 crowd-pleasing hockey movie, starring Ashton James as a promising young Black player, has a lot more on its mind than the original.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: ‘The Napa Boys’ Review: You Had Me at Merlot
item.description: This unclassifiable postmodern spoof, from the comedians Nick Corirossi and Armen Weitzman, is a strange, circuitous trip through California wine country.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh

item.title: ‘Heel’ Review: A Different Kind of House Training
item.description: A loutish teenager is shown the error of his ways in this absurdly gripping psychological thriller.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis

item.title: ‘André Is an Idiot’ Review: Dying Laughing
item.description: In this documentary, an ad man who treats life with irreverence tries to approach his death from colon cancer the same way.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘Dolly’ Review: Toy With Me
item.description: A backwoods monster with a twisted doll obsession puts a couple through the wringer in this unflinching exploitation fairy tale.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg
item.media:description: Max the Impaler, left, and Fabianne Therese in “Dolly.”

item.title: The Best Movies and TV Shows Coming to Disney+, Hulu, Apple TV and More in March
item.description: A new Bill Lawrence comedy starring Steve Carell and a new mystery starring Nicole Kidman are among this month’s highlights.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.media:description: Nicole Kidman plays a Virginia medical examiner in “Scarpetta,” based on the character created by Patricia Cornwell.

item.title: Watch a Haunting Theatrical Scene From ‘Hamnet’
item.description: The director Chloé Zhao narrates a sequence from her film featuring Jessie Buckley and Paul Mescal. The movie is nominated for eight Oscars, including best picture.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy
item.media:description: Jessie Buckley, center, in a scene from “Hamnet.”

item.title: The Many Brides of Frankenstein
item.description: Maggie Gyllenhaal’s “The Bride!” imagines an empowered mate for the monster. We look back at other memorable cinematic versions.
item.dc:creator: Robert Ito

item.title: ‘Hamnet’ | Anatomy of a Scene
item.description: The director Chloé Zhao narrates a sequence from her Oscar-nominated film, featuring Jessie Buckley and Paul Mescal.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy
item.media:description: Jessie Buckley, center, in a scene from “Hamnet.”
